claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation

Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let me start with the basics:

**Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children that I'd like to ask about - Alex, who's 6, and Blake, who's 3. They both live with me here in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know about Alex (age 6) and Blake (age 3), and that they both live with you in the UK.

Let me ask a few more questions to assess your eligibility:

**Are you currently responsible for both Alex and Blake's day-to-day care? By this I mean, are you the one providing for their needs such as food, clothes, and general upkeep?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. I look after both of them - I'm the one providing their food, clothes, and taking care of all their daily needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's great, thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just one more question to ensure I have all the information I need:

**Do either Alex or Blake live with someone else, or are they in hospital, care, or any other special living arrangement? Or do they live with you full-time?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives with me full-time, no issues there. But Blake... he's actually been in hospital for a few months now. He's been there for a while - it's been quite difficult.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Blake has been in hospital - that must be very challenging for your family.

To help assess your eligibility for Blake, I need to understand the situation a bit better:

**How long has Blake been in hospital? Has it been more or less than 12 weeks?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been more than 12 weeks - about 13 weeks now actually.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand this must be a difficult time.

When a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, there are some exceptions that might still allow you to continue receiving Child Benefit. One important exception is if you're regularly spending money on Blake while he's in hospital.

**Are you regularly spending money on Blake - for example, on things like clothes, toys, treats, or other items for him while he's in hospital?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, unfortunately I haven't been able to afford to spend money on his needs while he's in there. It's been really tough financially.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand, and I'm sorry to hear you're going through such a difficult time both emotionally and financially.

Just to make sure I have a complete picture before making my assessment:

**Is Blake in a UK hospital, and is there any other relevant information about your situation or the children that you think I should know?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Blake is in a UK hospital. No, I think that's everything - there's nothing else relevant that I can think of.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

## **For Alex (age 6): You ARE eligible ✓**

Alex lives with you full-time in the UK, you're responsible for their day-to-day care, they're under 16, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them. You meet all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

## **For Blake (age 3): You are NOT currently eligible ✗**

Unfortunately, because Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks (approximately 13 weeks), your entitlement to Child Benefit for Blake has been affected. Under the rules, Child Benefit normally stops after 12 weeks in hospital unless you're regularly spending money on the child while they're there. Since you've mentioned you haven't been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while he's in hospital, the exception doesn't apply in your situation.

---

**Important note:** You should contact HMRC about Blake's situation if you haven't already, as you're required to inform them when a child goes into hospital for more than 12 weeks. If Blake's circumstances change - for example, if he comes home or if you start regularly spending money on him - you may be able to claim Child Benefit for him again.

I'm sorry you're going through this difficult time. Is there anything else I can help clarify?

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old, lives with the claimant full-time in the UK, and the claimant is responsible for Alex's day-to-day care including providing food, clothes and general upkeep. Alex is under 16 years old, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. The claimant meets all the standard eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old and has been in a UK hospital for approximately 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week threshold. Under the Child Benefit rules, payments are normally affected after a child has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks. The exception that would allow continued Child Benefit is if the claimant is regularly spending money on the child while in hospital. However, the claimant has confirmed they have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while he's in hospital. Therefore, the claimant does not meet the exception criteria and is not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake after the 12-week period has passed.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (6) and Blake (3), both living in the UK with the claimant as their primary caregiver. For Alex, all standard eligibility criteria are met. For Blake, while he normally would be eligible, he has been in a UK hospital for more than 12 weeks (approximately 13 weeks). Under Child Benefit rules, entitlement usually ends after 12 weeks in hospital unless the claimant is regularly spending money on the child. Since the claimant cannot afford to spend money on Blake while he's in hospital, the exception does not apply, making them ineligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.
